data.title: xStocks' reduced SpaceX IPO allocation is not a sign of disrespect for the cryptocurrency market.
data.description:
SpaceX IPO subscription, RWA's attempt to redeem itself, ended somewhat disappointingly. Many cryptocurrency users didn't receive their SpaceX IPO allocations, but rather refunds.

Cryptocurrency IPO subscription platforms, including Binance, could have used tokenization to secure SpaceX IPO allocations for their users—given the epic scale of the IPO—but virtually all platforms failed to receive any allocations. The reason was issues with their supplier, xStocks.

xStocks originally intended to tokenize SpaceX IPO allocations as SPCXx, receiving over $1 billion in customer demand, indicating that this demand came primarily from these trading platforms. However, at the moment of actual allocation, the underlying shares available to upstream suppliers were insufficient, forcing the cancellation of many downstream activities. The platforms had limited options, only able to issue refunds and offer some compensation.

Given Bitcoin's current position among tech assets and the cryptocurrency community's place in the "potential hierarchy of contempt" within the secondary market, this event could easily be interpreted as "Wall Street looking down on the cryptocurrency community." They could simply cancel it at will. However, this interpretation is flawed. Because what's truly disrupted here isn't the on-chain minting process related to the cryptocurrency community, but rather the IPO allocation process. As the most watched SpaceX IPO globally, its core underwriters were Goldman Sachs and Morgan Stanley, with Bank of America, Citigroup, and JPMorgan also among the top underwriters. A top-tier, luxurious team. Another implication of this luxurious lineup is their immense influence. IPO allocation is inherently one of Wall Street's most powerful tools. Who gets the share, who gets a smaller share, and who gets only a symbolic amount—this isn't a matter of "technology neutrality." There are many precedents for last-minute changes. Not long ago, a similar situation occurred on Robinhood when Circle went public. Due to overwhelming demand, those who applied for Circle's IPO on Robinhood didn't receive a single share. Robinhood is considered the top retail investor platform in the US. Reddit's IPO filing also stated that shares were allocated to moderators, but they didn't receive a single share. Because of a 5-fold oversubscription, the moderators' share was taken by other platforms. Similar situations exist in other capital markets. Last year, the Economic Times reported that several foreign and Indian institutions withdrew from Meesho's anchor allotment in India, arguing that SBI Mutual Fund received too large a pre-IPO share. Institutions also fight amongst themselves. Let's look at the core underwriters, Goldman Sachs and Morgan Stanley, in this case. In 2005, the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) sued Morgan Stanley and Goldman Sachs, accusing the two companies of improper allocation practices during the underwriting of IPOs in 1999 and 2000. The SEC alleged that the two companies attempted to induce institutional clients who received IPO allocations to continue buying shares after the IPO. The two companies neither admitted nor denied the allegations, but each agreed to pay a $40 million civil penalty. Clearly, this wasn't the first time they had changed allocations at the last minute. An IPO isn't simply a matter of who first makes an offer and gets a share. Underwriters first collect intentions, then look at the order book, and then work with the issuer to decide who gets how much. There are too many soft commitments, channel expectations, and subscription intentions involved. What appears as "IPO allocation" on the trading platform's front end is merely a "potential allocation" on the investment bank's back end. Therefore, simply attributing the SPCXx IPO failure to xStocks' incompetence or the underwriting syndicate's disdain for the crypto market is inaccurate. xStocks certainly has issues with front-end expectation management. Users see "SpaceX IPO access" and feel they've participated in the IPO. However, if xStocks only receives subscription intentions, channel expectations, or non-final allocations upstream, it cannot create SpaceX shares out of thin air. Tokenization can only move assets on-chain; it cannot wrest IPO allocation rights from Goldman Sachs and Morgan Stanley. Crypto KOL @cryptobraveHQ mentioned the team behind xStocks. The core members, Adam Levi, Roberto Klein, and Yehonatan Goldman, are the former DAOStack team. Their previous project failed, and they later created Backed, which is now xStocks. The official website shows that Backed launched xStocks in 2025 with Kraken, Bybit, and Solana, reaching a product size of $150 million AUM and a cumulative trading volume exceeding $10 billion. However, this time it's likely unrelated to the crypto world; it's more like a traditional story. A hot IPO, explosive demand, tight underwriting books, and external channels being squeezed out. Previously, it was ordinary brokerage users and company community users who were squeezed out. Now it's xStocks and trading platform users' turn. It's just that the crypto world has once again run out of luck.

data.title: Is this an AI-driven subprime crisis? A $1.8 trillion exposure is becoming a ticking time bomb.
data.description:
Amid the AI ​​infrastructure construction boom, an unprecedented debt expansion is quietly taking shape—and the most dangerous part of it has never appeared on any balance sheet. Goldman Sachs' latest report predicts that by 2027, hyperscale cloud computing companies' capital expenditures will reach $1.1 trillion to $1.4 trillion, far exceeding market consensus. However, according to in-depth research by Morgan Stanley, this staggering figure is still just the tip of the iceberg. Nearly $1 trillion in procurement commitments, over $800 billion in outstanding leases, and tens of billions in supplier financing arrangements collectively constitute approximately $1.8 trillion in off-balance-sheet exposure—liabilities that exist off the balance sheet but effectively lock in future cash outflows. The market has not yet fully priced in these risks. Morgan Stanley warns that the leverage ratio of hyperscale cloud companies has surged from 0.9 to 1.8 in just two quarters, with capital expenditure growth consistently outpacing revenue and free cash flow growth, while the real impact of depreciation pressures has yet to arrive. Meanwhile, private lending institutions such as Apollo and Blackstone are using SPVs (Special Purpose Vehicles) to transfer leverage to the supply chain level, forming a highly cyclical and difficult-to-penetrate financing structure. If the commercialization of AI falls short of expectations, or if enterprise clients massively switch to cheaper alternatives, the vulnerability of the entire financing chain will be exposed. Debt Issuance Frenzy: AI Has Become the Biggest Variable in the Public Market. According to Morgan Stanley's latest "AI Debt Financing Tracking Report," as of the end of May 2026, the global issuance of AI-related bonds had reached $236 billion, a surge of 357% compared to the same period in 2025. Morgan Stanley predicts that the total issuance of AI debt for the year will exceed $570 billion, and the pace of issuance will further accelerate in the second half of the year as capital expenditure financing needs are concentrated. In April, AI-related bond issuance exceeded $74 billion, a new high for the year. Project financing structures (used for data center construction) accounted for 85% of high-yield bond supply and 40% of investment-grade bond supply. Meanwhile, five hyperscale cloud companies—Amazon, Meta, Google, Microsoft, and Oracle—currently account for 4% of the entire investment-grade bond index. In terms of leverage, the overall gross leverage ratio of hyperscale cloud companies has risen from 0.9 times in Q3 2025 to the current 1.8 times, increasing by approximately 0.3 times per quarter, exceeding the leverage level of the entire energy industry. Morgan Stanley points out that, due to supply pressures, related credit spreads have drifted from the AA range to the A range and may widen further. Meta's credit spread is currently wider than the CDX IG benchmark. Regarding free cash flow, Morgan Stanley predicts that Amazon and Meta's free cash flow will approach zero or even turn negative in 2026, at which point incremental financing will almost entirely rely on new debt.

$1.8 Trillion Off-Balance Sheet Exposure: Invisible Liabilities, Locked-Up Cash Outflows

Todd Castagno of Morgan Stanley's Global Valuation, Accounting & Tax team points out in a report that focusing solely on capital expenditure figures severely underestimates the true financial commitments of the AI ​​development cycle. Beyond disclosed capital expenditures, there are three key off-balance sheet exposures:

Purchase commitments of approximately $982 billion. Long-term purchase contracts totaling nearly $1 trillion from hyperscale cloud companies and Nvidia are close to $1 trillion. According to accounting standards, unless a company anticipates a loss on the contract, these obligations are not recognized as liabilities until the goods are delivered. Therefore, nearly $1 trillion in future cash outflows are not currently reflected as liabilities on any balance sheet.

It's worth noting that Nvidia's own inventory and procurement obligations have risen to approximately 32% of the consensus revenue forecast for fiscal year 2027, far exceeding the historical range of 15% to 20%, indicating that supply chain commitment risks have extended to chip suppliers. There are approximately $822 billion in outstanding lease commitments. Over $800 billion in lease contracts have been signed but not yet executed and are not included in current lease liabilities. Furthermore, arrangements such as variable lease payments, renewal options, and residual value guarantees are also off-balance sheet. Morgan Stanley estimates that if finance leases are included in the calculation, Microsoft's capital expenditures as a percentage of sales will jump from 33%/50% (fiscal years 2026/2027) to 44%/64%, while Oracle's may rise from 76%/115% to 101%/189%. Unpaid capital expenditures in accounts payable amount to approximately $110 billion. The significantly increased days on payable (DPO) for hyperscale cloud companies—Oracle up 370% year-on-year, Meta up 73%, and Microsoft up 69%—means that the entire supply chain is effectively funding AI development, with suppliers bearing the liquidity pressure that should be borne by the buyers. SPVs and Revolving Financing: Leverage Transferred to the Shadows

Another core dimension of off-balance-sheet risk is the revolving financing structure built through SPVs.

This week, Apollo and Blackstone jointly completed a $35 billion "chip-collateralized" private credit transaction for Anthropic, which vividly illustrates the operating logic of this model:

Broadcom provided the backing for the SPV, and Anthropic used the funds raised to purchase Google chips manufactured by Broadcom, while Google holds a 14% stake in Anthropic; Morgan Stanley, which arranged the transaction, also provided loans to investors participating in the transaction.

Morgan Stanley's AI ecosystem financing network diagram shows that OpenAI, Oracle, Nvidia, Microsoft, CoreWeave, AMD, and Amazon have multiple cyclical relationships involving customer, investor, and supplier financing and buybacks. The same funds circulate repeatedly among a few entities, with SPVs being the core tool for achieving this cycle. It is understood that Apollo's insurance subsidiary, Athene, is particularly active in this structure—raising funds by selling annuities to retirees and then injecting those funds into SPVs to participate in AI infrastructure financing. This model shifts leverage from the visible, massive cloud enterprise balance sheets to the supplier and private credit ecosystem, making the true systemic risk exposure difficult for external observers to identify and aggregate.

Depreciation Cliff and Monetization Gap: The Delayed Shock

Current financial data exhibits a systemic optimistic bias. A significant amount of capital expenditure is currently recorded as "Construction in Progress" (CIP), not yet depreciated, artificially inflating reported profit margins and underestimating future cost pressures. Oracle, Meta, and Google's CIP balances have increased by approximately 200%, 90%, and 55% year-over-year, respectively.

data.title: Cryptocurrency market collapses: Those heavily invested in SpaceX IPOs end up not even getting a share.
data.description:
Author: Sanqing, Foresight News

On June 12th, SpaceX went public on Nasdaq with an IPO price of $135 per share, ticker symbol SPCX, valuing the company at approximately $1.77 trillion, setting a new record for the largest IPO in history. According to Nasdaq data, SPCX reached a high of $176.52 that day, closing at $160.95, a 19.22% increase from the IPO price, with a total trading volume of approximately 511 million shares.

However, what truly ignited the crypto community that day was not the opening price, but the refund announcements issued by various platforms, almost all of which failed to secure SpaceX stock.

Seven platforms, seven responses

Kraken: The core initiator of this IPO activity.

Through its parent company's xStocks platform, the IPO was open to users in over 110 countries, touting the concept of "the world's first IPO with 24/7 continuous trading." In the week leading up to the listing, it attracted over $1 billion in subscription demand. On the listing day, a single account was allocated a maximum of only 4.2786 shares, with a total cost of approximately $606.50. Bybit: The official announcement was direct: "Due to xStocks' inability to deliver the underlying assets, it failed to obtain any SpaceX IPO allocation." All subscription funds were 100% refunded, with an additional 4 days of compensation interest calculated at a 10% annualized rate. Bitget Wallet: Also experiencing a missed quota, a full refund was issued, along with a 10 USDT GetGas subsidy. However, the refund process itself sparked secondary controversy: users subscribed using USDC, but the refunds were uniformly deposited into USDT on the Solana chain, causing strong dissatisfaction due to the price difference. Xie Jiayin, Bitget's Chinese-language head, publicly apologized on X, admitting improper handling and promising to reimburse the difference and gas fees. Binance Wallet: Announced the cancellation of the SPCXx IPO event, with a full refund. The compensation plan involves distributing 1 million SPCXB tokens (equivalent to SpaceX shares in Binance's own bStocks product) equally among participating users. This effectively promoted bStocks. MEXC: Received notification from its partner that it was ultimately unable to provide any allocations, and is offering a full refund along with the following compensation: a refund of the activity task fee within 24 hours, a 2-day 6% APR flexible wealth management USDT interest rate increase coupon, and an SPCX USDT contract position airdrop coupon with a maximum value of 500 USDT. OKX: Did not participate in the xStocks SPCXx IPO collaboration, and was later jokingly referred to by netizens as "winning by default." Gate: Completed the allocation relatively well, due to differences in infrastructure. Gate partnered with Alpaca on June 3rd. Alpaca is a licensed self-clearing broker in the US, possessing formal brokerage status and able to directly participate in the IPO allocation process. Gate entered the underwriters' allocation chain through Alpaca, rather than through an additional intermediary layer like xStocks. Ultimately, it completed the allocation of 5% to 12% of the shares proportionally, and the SPCX shares of the allocated users went directly into their Gate stock accounts. Why was the allocation announcement delayed until after the market opened? Kraken employee Nata tweeted at 3:00 PM about a "distribution delay," but it wasn't until 10:00 PM, some time after the auction began, that Nata provided a more detailed explanation of the allocation issue: the Kraken xStocks platform failed to obtain the expected pre-IPO allocation from the SpaceX underwriters, and user orders were only partially fulfilled. Nata explained that IPO allocations are entirely determined by the issuer's underwriters and are unrelated to Kraken or xStocks; global demand for this offering was exceptionally high. All unfulfilled orders would be fully refunded to user accounts without any fees. However, she subsequently deleted the tweet. Let's first outline the allocation chain for a US IPO. SpaceX adopted a fixed offering price model, locking in $135 per share before the roadshow, with the subscription window closing on June 11. Subsequently, lead underwriters such as Goldman Sachs and Morgan Stanley dominated the allocation: priority was given to core institutional investors (hedge funds, mutual funds, pension funds), followed by registered brokers with direct agreements with the underwriters, and finally retail investors. Underwriters do not directly settle shares with non-registered entities; each allocation must correspond to a transaction party registered with FINRA or the SEC. It's worth noting that SpaceX's overall retail allocation was reportedly reduced from approximately 30% to just over 20%, with even users of traditional retail brokers like Fidelity and Robinhood being proportionally reduced. xStocks itself does not possess the qualifications of a US-registered broker, meaning it cannot appear directly on the underwriters' allocation list under its own name and must go through a licensed intermediary. How much allocation this intermediary received in this IPO, and how much it decided to allocate to xStocks, is unknown. A rumor circulating online suggests that xStocks, seeing SPCX's high opening price, withheld its shares; if the opening price were lower than the offering price, it would have allocated all shares to users. This conspiracy theory is indeed very appealing. However, a more reasonable explanation might be that, given the largest IPO in history, xStocks, as an upstream distributor for Kraken, Bybit, Binance Wallet, Bitget Wallet, and MEXC, received a very limited allocation from its intermediary brokerage layer. After xStocks' overall quota was drastically reduced, Kraken, a platform directly under its parent company Payward, received only a small portion of the quota, while other platforms received nothing through xStocks. One entry point does not equal a real stock. Some users had opened short positions in the SPCX contract market in advance to hedge, originally planning to close their positions and lock in the price difference after receiving the spot shares. However, the spot shares failed to materialize, while the short positions continued to incur losses as SPCX rose from $135 to over $160. They lost money on both the IPO subscription fees and the price difference hedging. This incident exposed not only the execution problems of a single platform, but also the vulnerability of the "platform fundraising to secure quotas from upstream suppliers" model. The multi-layered agency aggregation method is essentially no different from traditional investment intermediaries; the longer the chain, the weaker the ability to realize profits under extreme pressure. The failure of xStocks vividly illustrates this hidden danger. On the same day, Gate.io achieved true proportional distribution (5%-12%) through Alpaca (a licensed self-clearing broker in the US), while xStocks' multi-level marketing model saw significant order cuts and even zero allocations on multiple platforms. This demonstrates that the dividing line is never about "whether or not tokenization exists," but rather whether the supply chain can truly deliver on its promises under extreme circumstances. This incident has provided a vivid lesson for all users participating in crypto ICOs and offers clear reference standards for future platform selection: direct access to the compliance qualifications of licensed brokers, transparent and verifiable quota sources and allocation priorities, and a clear distinction between "true ownership" and "price exposure."

data.title: Raydium Stolen: New DeFi Risks Lurk in Forgotten Old Contracts
data.description:
Author: Gino Matos; Translator: Luffy, Foresight News

TL;DR:

  • Hackers stole approximately $1.34 million in assets using Raydium's long-deprecated V3 Automated Market Maker (AMM) liquidity pool.

  • This incident exposes a common problem: decommissioned DeFi projects' old contracts are still running normally on-chain. These forgotten underlying infrastructures have become easily overlooked targets for attacks.

  • Public reports show that since March 2025, there have been at least eight similar incidents of old contract theft in the industry, meaning that a large amount of neglected old code can still be called externally.

  • Recently, a vulnerability in Raydium AMM V3 caused a loss of $1.34 million. The project was linked to five liquidity pools outside the current product ecosystem. These pools were not supported by Raydium's UI or SDK and were inaccessible to ordinary users, yet they were still exploited by hackers. This attack targeted obsolete contracts and underlying infrastructure that were largely ignored in the industry, exposing a major vulnerability in the full lifecycle management of smart contracts. This type of problem is not limited to this decentralized exchange within the Solana ecosystem. Ignored Risk Categories According to publicly available security incident reports, since March 2025, there have been at least eight cases of attacks specifically targeting obsolete, outdated, or obsolete contracts, with a total loss of approximately $10.8 million. If we include security incidents caused by outdated funding pools and older supporting products in the statistics, the number of related incidents reaches 10 (including the Raydium theft), with a total loss of approximately $22.5 million. Currently, most security incident tracking platforms in the industry categorize attacks according to their technical causes, with common classifications including: smart contract code vulnerabilities, ineffective access control, oracle tampering, private key leaks, and cross-chain bridge defects. Zombie contracts (i.e., old contracts that the project has announced are no longer in use but can still be called normally on the chain) represent a completely different risk dimension. These are security incidents caused by problems in contract lifecycle management, yet they are consistently buried in the statistics of various conventional vulnerabilities and are not classified separately. Raydium's V3 automated market maker liquidity pool was abandoned because the Serum project it relied on officially shut down, causing the old contract to completely lose its original functionality, and the corresponding liquidity assets remained idle on the chain. Raydium's current new contract double-checks two key pieces of information: first, it verifies the asset proportion through a total supply verification mechanism; second, it verifies the minting address of the liquidity tokens and various associated account information. However, the old V3 contract completely omits these two verification processes. Hackers exploited this vulnerability to forge new liquidity tokens and impersonate legitimate credentials, directly bypassing all risk control rules. In this incident, approximately 150,177 RAY, 5,603 SOL, and 893,700 USDC were stolen. These assets had been stored in the platform's old liquidity pool for a long time. Although they were no longer part of the mainstream business, their on-chain access was never disabled. Eight cases expose common problems. Since 2025, many well-known DeFi projects have stumbled on old contracts. All the incidents share the same characteristics: the project team claims that the current version of the product and active users are unaffected, but because the old contracts were not completely shut down, the project's treasury ultimately bears all the losses.

    Why are the risks of old contracts overlooked?

    Currently, most security incident classification systems in the industry focus on attack methods, tampered objects, and code failures, which is an analytical perspective of "starting from technical vulnerabilities." This also leads to the concealment of zombie contract incidents. The core of this problem is never a coding error, but rather that the project should have completely shut down the old contracts, but failed to do so.

    A 2025 industry research paper summarized 50 major global crypto security incidents between 2022 and 2025, with cumulative losses exceeding $1 billion.

    Research indicates that high-risk on-chain attacks are often the result of a chain of overlapping risks, simultaneously involving multiple levels such as human operation, daily maintenance, economic models, contract lifecycle, and community governance. The paper proposes a four-layer root cause analysis framework, explicitly classifying contract lifecycle management vulnerabilities, community governance vulnerabilities, and code writing vulnerabilities as independent risk categories. The zombie contract problem is a typical lifecycle management vulnerability. However, in the existing security statistics system, such incidents are all categorized as "code vulnerabilities," and the corresponding loss data is hidden under other categories, failing to attract sufficient attention from the industry. Beware of "Contract Graveyards": Outdated facilities have become new attack hotspots. If DeFi projects consistently treat "contract shutdown" as a trivial matter, merely marking "this contract is deactivated" in product documentation without transferring idle assets, disabling call functions, or continuously monitoring the status, then hackers will continue to target this "contract graveyard." The historical deployment records of every large DeFi project have now become searchable and exploitable targets for hackers. The currently estimated $22.5 million in losses only represents publicly exposed cases; the true risk is far higher. Those outdated fund pools, historical authorized interfaces, and early collaborative modules that hold assets but are detached from mainstream user workflows receive far less operational monitoring than current business systems, making them prime targets for hackers. To change this situation, firstly, "zombie contracts" must be classified as an independent risk category and their incidents recorded separately; secondly, the contract decommissioning process must be incorporated into standardized security procedures and given equal importance to code auditing. Only by implementing comprehensive lifecycle maintenance can the attack scope be effectively reduced. Currently, the industry's handling methods are largely similar. Raydium used its project treasury to pay out $1.34 million in losses, while Transit Finance and Huma Finance also had their project teams bear the user losses. This also means that contract decommissioning is no longer just a documentation task, but an essential security control step. Seven Security Control Standards for Contract Decommissioning For the closure of old contracts, the industry can establish standardized control procedures, with specific requirements and functions as follows: Simply marking "Contract is decommissioned" in the documentation only transfers the security risk to the project treasury; the attack vulnerability still exists. Simply announcing the product's decommissioning without a complete technical shutdown allows old contracts to remain accessible indefinitely: project teams are negligent in their oversight, while hackers remain ever-watchful. The value of DeFi projects lies not only in their current asset lock-up size but also in their historical code and underlying architecture. This forgotten history has now become a new security vulnerability.

data.title: Breaking: US government bans foreigners from using Claude Fable 5
data.description:
Anthropic's latest models, Fable 5 and Mythos 5, are embroiled in renewed controversy.

According to a report by Axios, the Trump administration has formally invoked national security powers to impose extremely strict export controls on Anthropic's most advanced AI models—Fable 5 and Mythos 5—completely prohibiting access to them from foreign governments, companies, and individuals.

This undoubtedly indicates that the United States is escalating its control over cutting-edge AI systems and viewing them as national security assets. Axios reports that U.S. Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick sent a letter to Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei on Friday, stating that the Mythos 5 and Fable 5 models will be subject to export controls, covering any location outside the United States and all foreigners within the United States. In other words, this isn't simply a matter of "banning overseas users from subscribing to Claude," but rather placing cutting-edge models within an export control framework similar to that of high-end chips or sensitive software. According to a government official, this move was made because the U.S. Department of Commerce learned that another company claimed to have successfully jailbroken Mythos, an incident that raised concerns about potential national security risks. Previously, before Anthropic released its latest model, the U.S. government attempted to halt its release but failed, leading to this export control letter. Reuters reported earlier this month that Trump signed an executive order requiring leading AI developers to voluntarily submit their most advanced models to the government for cybersecurity testing before releasing them to the public. The official stated that these models must remain blocked until the U.S. government's national security system is fully fortified, which may be completed in the coming weeks. This means that the export, re-export, or transfer of the Anthropic model to foreign entities within the United States requires a license. Furthermore, Anthropic must submit additional applications to obtain a license that is verified on a per-item basis. Failure to comply with these requirements will result in both financial and civil penalties for Anthropic. Currently, Anthropic has issued a statement regarding its control measures: [Image of image] Combined with previous news of Anthropic being added to the Pentagon's blacklist, Anthropic is currently in a "delicate situation": On the one hand, it was added to the Pentagon's blacklist because it was considered too dangerous and unsuitable for use by the US government; on the other hand, Anthropic... It has also been included in the Ministry of Commerce's licensing and control system because it is considered too dangerous and unsuitable for foreign use... What do you think?

data.title: The trillion-dollar bell tolls for Musk; crypto investors' dream of investing in SpaceX is shattered.
data.description:
Author: Nancy, PANews

After more than two decades of waiting, Musk has finally brought SpaceX to the capital markets.

“It’s hard to believe that a company that started as a small warehouse in El Segundo is now having the biggest IPO in history. We hope that in the future, anyone who wants to go to the moon, anyone who wants to go to Mars, or even any corner of the solar system, will have the opportunity to do so. Someday, we hope to be able to take you there, not just a few astronauts. Whoever you are, SpaceX hopes to be able to take you to the moon, take you to Mars, and ultimately go beyond that. I am now very confident that with this amazing team at SpaceX, we will make all of this a reality in the near future.” Musk said via video link from Starbase headquarters in Texas at the SpaceX Nasdaq bell-ringing ceremony.

On the evening of June 12, SpaceX officially rang the Nasdaq opening bell, with the stock code "SPCX". By the close of trading, SpaceX's stock price had continued its strong upward trend, rising more than 30% intraday from its IPO price of $135, ultimately closing up 19.22% at $160.95, giving it a total market capitalization of approximately $2.1 trillion, making it the sixth largest company by market capitalization in the United States. This space giant, which had suffered three failed rocket launches and faced near-depleted cash flow, repeatedly pushed Musk to the brink of desperation. Now, it has successfully entered the public market with a record-breaking valuation, not only setting a new record for the world's largest IPO but also opening a new chapter for the commercial space industry. Global capital collectively "rushed for rockets," while crypto users were left "running alongside" in the IPO. On the eve of its listing, Wall Street had already entered SpaceX time. For this landmark IPO, SpaceX assembled a stellar underwriting syndicate, with 23 Wall Street investment banks participating. Goldman Sachs served as the lead underwriter, with Morgan Stanley, Bank of America, Citigroup, and JPMorgan Chase among the collaborators. To attract clients, executives from several investment banks personally led roadshows, targeting ultra-wealthy and institutional clients. Goldman Sachs and Bank of America even displayed rocket models in their headquarters lobbies, and Elon Musk's mother, Maye Musk, attended JPMorgan Chase's roadshow. Market estimates suggest the IPO could generate over $500 million in underwriting revenue for the banks. Analysts point out that these investment banks not only hope to facilitate SpaceX's successful listing but also anticipate boosting market confidence and creating a more favorable market environment for subsequent large IPOs such as OpenAI and Anthropic. From its long-term vision of multi-planetary civilizations to its massive commercialization of orbital AI, SpaceX's fervent roadshow has ignited investors' imaginations, drawing a global influx of capital. Multiple media outlets, citing market sources, reported that SpaceX's IPO received over $250 billion in subscription demand, far exceeding its planned fundraising of approximately $75 billion, representing an oversubscription rate of nearly four times. Institutional funds were the main force behind the IPO subscription, with approximately 1,000 institutional investors participating. BlackRock reportedly placed a single subscription exceeding $5 billion; Middle Eastern sovereign wealth funds such as the Saudi Public Investment Fund (PIF) and the Kuwait Investment Authority (KIA) also submitted subscription orders worth billions of dollars. Retail investor enthusiasm was equally high. Bloomberg, citing sources familiar with the matter, reported that retail investor demand exceeded $100 billion, far exceeding the allocation size for retail investors. Based on the current issuance arrangements, it is expected that most retail investors' subscription applications will not receive a full allocation, or even any allocation at all. Looking at the subscription results for crypto investors, the actual allocation was extremely limited, with almost all applicants failing and only a few receiving a minimum guarantee number. Kraken basically allocated approximately 4.2786 SPCXx shares per person, equivalent to about $600 USD. Regardless of the subscription amount, it was a universal allocation, with any remaining funds fully refunded. Gate uses a pro-rata allocation, with a success rate of approximately 3% of the subscription amount, slightly better than other platforms. Binance announced a full refund and additionally purchased $1 million worth of SPCXB tokens for airdrops, averaging about $40 USD per person. Bitget also provided a full refund and issued a $10 USD transaction fee coupon as compensation. Bybit, in addition to refunds, also compensated users with an extra 10% annualized investment return for their deposited funds over four days. Users who had enthusiastically participated in the early stages ultimately ended up with nothing, receiving only a full refund or symbolic compensation, sparking dissatisfaction. Some IPO groups even turned into protest groups. The main reason for this phenomenon is that most exchanges rely on xStocks as the underlying asset provider, and the subscription demand from institutional and retail investors in this round significantly exceeded the underwriting quota, leading to a substantial reduction in orders on many platforms, with some even being canceled. However, this still did not dampen the enthusiasm for SpaceX. During the SPCX opening phase, trading did not proceed as scheduled at 9:30 AM Eastern Time, but was delayed. This is mainly due to the special mechanism of such mega-IPOs. Exchanges typically initiate an opening cross-market auction process to centrally match buy and sell orders, balance supply and demand, and determine a reasonable opening price. This has also occurred in large IPOs such as Meta, Alibaba, and Figma. In fact, to cope with the potential historically high trading volume and order processing pressure, Nasdaq, Citadel Securities, Jane Street, and S&P Global, among other institutions, have conducted multiple rounds of internal drills and system stress tests weeks in advance. S&P Global stated that it has increased its processing capacity by approximately 200% through system upgrades and real-time testing over the past six weeks, indicating that this is a special preparation to cope with the unprecedented trading volume, and its level of preparation is rare even in previous large IPO projects. Early-stage venture capitalists made a fortune, and even cafeteria workers experienced a windfall. Following SpaceX's trillion-dollar IPO, an unprecedented wave of wealth realization ensued. For early investors who have accompanied SpaceX through its long growth cycle, this IPO has brought historic returns. For example, Google's SpaceX shares are expected to be worth over $100 billion; hedge fund D1 Capital Partners' shares are expected to be worth approximately $20 billion; legendary investor Ron Baron has participated in 27 rounds of SpaceX financing over the years, and his shareholding has risen to approximately $12 billion; while Andreessen Horowitz, a well-known Silicon Valley venture capital firm, is expected to receive its largest investment return since the company's inception, with its shareholding value expected to exceed $10 billion. University endowments in the United States have also benefited from this wealth feast. For example, approximately 10% of the University of North Carolina System's endowment has exposure to SpaceX; Washington University in St. Louis holds more than 15% of its SpaceX-related assets; and Stanford University also holds a significant share in SpaceX. In addition to institutional investors, SpaceX employees have also received substantial returns. SpaceX currently employs approximately 22,000 people, plus hundreds of former employees. According to an analysis by investment platform Hill, about 400 current and former employees hold shares worth over $100 million, becoming a new generation of billionaires. Besides Elon Musk becoming the world's first trillionaire, SpaceX COO Gwynne Shotwell and CFO Bret Johnsen's shares are both expected to be worth over $1 billion. SpaceX director Antonio Gracias, a director and founder of Valor Equity Partners, holds shares worth approximately $68 billion; another director, Luke Nosek, holds shares worth approximately $5 billion. More than 4,400 employees became millionaires in this IPO. From rocket welders and manufacturing engineers to administrative staff and cafeteria workers, many have shared in the company's growth. According to the WSJ, many initially received their stock at a price of less than $2 per share, before SpaceX had even achieved rocket recovery. As the company underwent multiple rounds of financing, stock splits, and continuous valuation increases, the value of their shares also grew significantly. For example, former welder Juan Hernandez joined SpaceX in 2015 at a wage of $28 per hour and initially received approximately $10,000 in stock incentives. Although he sold some shares when the company's valuation reached approximately $36 billion, his remaining shares are still worth about $880,000. A former SpaceX engineer received stock incentives years ago, which are now worth over $28 million. However, many employees missed out on even greater potential for appreciation by selling their shares too early. According to the WSJ, some sold shares to pay off their spouses' student loans, some used the proceeds to buy vacation homes for their parents, and others invested the funds in their own startups. These pragmatic choices prevented them from realizing wealth increases of tens or even hundreds of times. Is the trillion-dollar space story really worth it? A company still burning through cash has become the world's most expensive IPO, and Musk's market valuation has sparked debate. From a financial perspective, SpaceX is far from reaching a mature, profitable stage. The prospectus shows that in the first quarter of this year, the company's revenue increased by 15% year-on-year to $4.69 billion, but the net loss for the quarter was still a staggering $4.28 billion. Since its founding in 2002, the company has accumulated losses of approximately $41.3 billion and frankly admitted in its prospectus that it may not be able to achieve sustained profitability in the future. However, supporters believe that traditional profit models are insufficient to measure SpaceX's value. In their view, SpaceX is not just a rocket launch company, but a platform enterprise integrating satellite internet, artificial intelligence, and future space infrastructure; its true value comes from its growth potential over the next few decades. For example, technology industry analyst Daniel Newman stated that if investors take a five-year view, SpaceX's performance will be outstanding. The offering price of $135 per share may seem expensive a year from now, but it will likely be considered quite cheap five years from now. He revealed that he plans to make a small purchase on SpaceX's first day of trading to avoid missing out entirely due to short-term misjudgment; however, he also anticipates that a more ideal time to build a position may emerge in the first 12 months after the IPO. Currently, Oppenheimer has given SpaceX an "outperform" rating and set a target price of $190 over the next 12 to 18 months, representing approximately 40% upside from the IPO price of $135. New Street Research also gave an optimistic assessment, initiating coverage with a target price of $165, corresponding to approximately 22% upside. According to sources, SpaceX disclosed to investors that it has received investment-grade ratings from three major bond rating agencies, which may help it reduce costs when raising funds after the IPO. However, there are also many doubts in the market. Valuation guru Aswath... Damodaran believes SpaceX's current pricing is too high and he won't buy immediately. He predicts a repeat of the significant pullbacks seen with Facebook and Uber after the IPO (both experiencing drops of over 50%), advising patience and waiting for a lower entry point. He notes that while the AI ​​business makes SpaceX's story more ambitious, it also increases volatility, potentially putting pressure on its short-term performance. Wall Street commentator Jim Cramer believes the main risk of SpaceX's IPO isn't undersubscription, but rather the potential for short-term investors to cash out in the early stages of the listing, amplifying stock price fluctuations. Veteran short seller James Chanos stated that SpaceX's highly anticipated IPO is driven more by investor enthusiasm for Elon Musk and ZAI than by financial fundamentals. He argues that the company's valuation is difficult to justify based on any reasonable business assumptions, making it an IPO propped up by hope and dreams. On one hand, there's the financial reality of massive losses; on the other, the grand vision of reaching for the stars. As a pioneer in the space economy, SpaceX has no comparable precedent, and its future remains uncertain. With the trillion-dollar mark already tolled, the market is beginning to price the space dream.

data.title: SpaceX's IPO debacle: Another lesson for the crypto world.
data.description:
Author: Little Bear Biscuit; Source: BitpushNews

On June 12th, SpaceX officially listed on Nasdaq.

How large? It raised approximately $75 billion, with a valuation reaching $1.75 trillion—the largest IPO in human history, bar none. The offering price was $135, and it took off immediately upon opening.

Traditional brokerages were scrambling to participate in this star stock. What about retail investors in the cryptocurrency market? They weren't originally eligible, but several CEXs said, "We have tokenized stocks (or direct connections to US stocks), you can participate in the IPO using USDT."

Major exchanges also did a lot of pre-launch hype and marketing, but what happened?

It all backfired.

It was a disaster.

The core conflict of the entire incident points to xStocks. On the day of the listing, xStocks' upstream underwriters were unable to deliver the full amount of SpaceX's underlying shares to participating platforms due to a significant reduction in retail allocations. Several exchanges, including Bybit and Binance, which partnered with xStocks, subsequently announced that they could not complete the settlement and would only issue collective refunds. Among the major centralized exchanges (CEXs), Gate may emerge as the biggest winner. According to its official announcement, Gate operates independently through its own IPO Access/Pre-IPOs channel and does not rely on third-party platforms such as xStocks. The total allocation was approximately 33,900 SPCX (worth approximately $20 million), distributed using a transparent rule that combines time weighting and capital allocation. Platform announcements indicate that after the subscription period ends (June 12th), the asset certificates are expected to be directly distributed to users' Gate Stock accounts around 14:00–15:00 UTC, supporting immediate trading. User feedback generally confirms that they "received the goods immediately," indicating a high rate of fulfillment. The following are the IPO subscription results and "cleanup" plans of other major cryptocurrency exchanges: Kraken received the most criticism from the community. It received far less allocation from underwriters than expected. While some user orders were filled, each person only received approximately 4.28 shares (worth about $600). Regardless of whether you were a large investor or a small investor, everyone was treated equally. Many users exclaimed, "It would have been better not to participate at all!" Binance: Full refunds, then personally invests $1 million to airdrop SPCXB tokens from the bStocks ecosystem to each participant (approximately 27,000), averaging about $37-40 per person. A smart move. Bybit: Zero distribution, full refund of principal, plus an extra 4 days of interest (11 yuan for $10,000). Not amazing, but not bad either. Bitget: Cancelled, full refund + small compensation such as gas vouchers, but the problem is that the refund is in USDT instead of the original asset, and the price difference is deducted. Also, a mention of OKX: OKX did not participate in this spot/IPO product, only providing derivative contracts, perfectly avoiding all the pitfalls. It has to be said, Lao Xu once again wrote "The Compliance Life" correctly :) Why can't retail investors in the crypto world get quotas? This mishap, on the surface, is that xStocks, the upstream service provider, "dropped the ball" at a crucial moment, but looking deeper, it is actually a "dimensional reduction attack" of the traditional financial compliance and clearing system on the crude tokenization narrative of the crypto world. US IPO allocation is a centralized game highly monopolized by the "lead underwriter" and driven by profit. According to SEC (Securities and Exchange Commission) underwriting regulations, new share allocations are tightly controlled by lead underwriters such as Goldman Sachs and Morgan Stanley. Top-tier licensed brokers like Fidelity, being part of the underwriting syndicate, directly receive first-hand orders and can naturally support retail investors in subscribing to new shares; while ordinary brokers not on the list can only obtain second or third-hand allocations. In extreme cases like SpaceX, where oversubscription is several times higher than expected, underwriters will only prioritize allocating to top institutions and core clients. The final asset transfer must be strictly cleared through the DTC (Depository & Clearing Corporation) and compliant accounts of licensed clearing brokers. Binance, Bybit, and other cryptocurrency exchanges are not licensed clearinghouses in the US stock market. The xStocks platform they connect to is essentially a middleman "grouping" transactions outside the compliance firewall. When traditional Wall Street's major funds are insufficient even for their own allocations, underwriters will directly cut off the quotas of these secondary channels. Without clearing sovereignty, these middlemen are not even qualified to enter the market in the face of Wall Street's top-level clearing walls, and downstream cryptocurrency exchanges are naturally forced to refund. Returning to the recent RWA (Rich Democracy and Investment) craze for tokenized US stocks, the current crypto market desperately craves anchoring itself to genuine, high-quality hard assets. However, this incident exposes a core problem: tokenized stocks ≠ you actually own the stocks. Legally, these tokenized products are "shadow derivatives." Where does the quota come from? How is it held in cross-border custody? How are the tokens mapped? How is it finally delivered? If any link in this chain goes wrong, retail investors can only wait for a refund. Even worse, your name won't appear on SpaceX's shareholder register. You have no voting rights, and dividends are not protected by Delaware corporate law. You think you're buying the future of spaceships, but you're actually just holding a worthless IOU that can be torn up at any time outside the high walls of compliance. If RWA truly wants to be implemented, it needs to connect these intermediary chains first. Otherwise, it will continue to pay the price for its "tuition fees." Blockchain wanting to penetrate traditional finance? It's probably still a long way off.

On June 13, the US CFTC filed a lawsuit in the Federal District Court for the District of New Mexico against Governor Michelle Lujan Grisham, Attorney General Raúl Torrez, and other officials, attempting to prevent the state from applying gambling regulations to prediction market platforms. Previously, New Mexico sued Kalshi, accusing it of offering sports betting to state residents without a license and allowing users under the state's legal gambling age of 21 to participate. The New Mexico Attorney General stated that legal gambling in the state can only operate under tribal and state gambling agreements or strict state regulations. The CFTC, however, argues that platforms like Kalshi offer federally regulated derivatives contracts, not gambling products under state law. CFTC Chairman Michael Selig stated that New Mexico is attempting to impose its state betting laws on federal derivatives exchanges under the exclusive jurisdiction of the CFTC. In recent months, the CFTC has filed lawsuits against several states, including Wisconsin, Illinois, Arizona, Connecticut, and New York, to establish its regulatory authority over the sports prediction market. This week, the agency also proposed broader prediction market rules, which would generally still allow sports-related contracts, indicating an escalating conflict between federal and state governments over the boundaries of prediction markets and sports betting.

▌ Attorneys General from Multiple US States Launch Joint Investigation into OpenAI

Multiple sources familiar with the matter said that the artificial intelligence company received a broad subpoena on Friday, requiring it to submit various operational and user impact documents, covering advertising business, user activity and retention, consumer and health data management, business related to minors and the elderly, deep learning models, model flattery issues, and internal company rules and regulations. An OpenAI spokesperson stated, "Artificial intelligence is an emerging and powerful technology, and we are committed to ensuring the public can safely enjoy the benefits of technology in a compliant and secure manner. We value the concerns raised by state attorneys general and will actively cooperate with relevant departments." IBM will receive $1 billion to build a quantum-level wafer fab, GlobalFoundries will receive $375 million, and the remaining funds will be allocated to seven quantum hardware companies.

On June 13th, according to on-chain analyst Yu Jin's monitoring, the largest SPCX long position on Hyperliquid has essentially closed out its SPCX long positions, ultimately incurring a loss of $300,000 instead of making a profit. After the listing, the SPCX price hovered around this price without further increase, and the investor eventually closed out its positions over the past few hours at an average price of $169.8, resulting in a loss of $300,000.

The Times of Israel believes that Katz's remarks suggest that Israel may again use force to prevent Iran from acquiring nuclear weapons.
