▌US Treasury traders lower their bets on a 2026 Fed rate hike

On June 13, as market optimism grew regarding the end of the war with Iran, US Treasury traders bet that the Federal Reserve would raise interest rates early next year as the week drew to a close. On Friday afternoon, interest rate swap transactions showed that traders now expect a 100% probability of a 25 basis point rate hike by January 2027; previously, they had bet on a rate hike this year. This shift came after data showed that US core inflation accelerated at a relatively moderate pace last month, US crude oil prices fell below $85 a barrel, and US President Donald Trump declared progress on a peace agreement with Iran.

“Signs of a possible reopening of the Strait of Hormuz and falling oil prices have been key narratives driving the market,” said Dhiraj Narula, U.S. interest rate strategist at HSBC Securities. “These factors have, to some extent, delayed expectations of when interest rate hikes will occur.” U.S. bank deposits fell to $19.296 trillion from $19.338 trillion last week.

▌Sources: US Military Shoots Down Multiple Iranian One-Way Attack Drones Heading Towards the Strait of Hormuz

June 13, according to Reuters: A source revealed that the US military shot down multiple Iranian one-way attack drones heading towards the Strait of Hormuz. The source stated that Iranian drones posed a threat to commercial shipping. (Jinshi)

    Golden Encyclopedia

    ▌Can Bitcoin Still Be Called "Digital Gold" Amid Geopolitical Crises?

    Every time a geopolitical crisis occurs, gold prices rise while Bitcoin prices plummet. After six tests, the claim of "digital gold" has never been confirmed by data. Countries hoard gold but exclude Bitcoin from their reserves.

    For investors, Bitcoin exhibits an asymmetry: it falls with stocks but doesn't rise with them. Three structural asymmetries prevent Bitcoin from achieving safe-haven status: an overabundance of derivatives (market structure), the dominance of leveraged traders (participant composition), and a lack of repeatable behavioral records (behavioral accumulation). Bitcoin is not a safe-haven asset, but it is a "crisis-useful asset," capable of functioning when borders are closed and banks fail. If these three asymmetries narrow, Bitcoin may no longer be a replica of gold, but rather a completely new "next-generation gold." Generational shifts and the widespread adoption of algorithms are key factors that could accelerate this process.

data.title: In-depth analysis of SpaceX's IPO: Is the company worth $2.5 trillion?
data.description:
I completely missed last year's commercial space boom because my first job was at an institute of the Chinese Academy of Sciences, working on satellite R&D. Although I later switched to semiconductors, I continued to follow this area, believing progress wouldn't be as rapid, but the market experienced a major boom. Today, SpaceX is about to go public. Although Elon Musk is known for his outlandish pronouncements, SpaceX already has mature technology and a market presence. The offering price is $135 per share, and Oppenheimer has given it a target price of $190, corresponding to a company valuation of approximately $2.5 trillion. Many people will ask: Is this company really worth that much money?

To answer this question, we need to understand what SpaceX is doing. This is not an ordinary space company, nor simply a satellite communications service provider, nor just another AI startup. SpaceX is attempting to establish dominance in three areas simultaneously: launch services, global satellite communications, and artificial intelligence.

Moreover, these three businesses are not simply pieced together, but rather a meticulously designed system, with each part supporting the others. In a recent interview, Elon Musk spent considerable time discussing the "Kardashev Scale"—a framework for measuring the progress of civilizations using energy utilization capabilities. This framework divides civilizations into three levels: Type I civilizations can utilize the energy of an entire planet, Type II civilizations can utilize the energy of a star, and Type III civilizations can utilize the energy of an entire galaxy. What is humanity's current state? We are far from even reaching Type I civilization. Earth receives only about one five-hundred-millionth of the sun's total output, and the solar energy we utilize is less than one trillionth of that. The sun accounts for 99.86% of the solar system's mass; of the remaining 0.14%, most is Jupiter. On this scale, Earth is merely a "tiny speck of dust." Musk's point is straightforward: if humanity wants to make progress at the Kardashev scale, it must go to space. Utilizing even a millionth of the sun's energy requires deploying massive amounts of solar energy collection facilities in space. To achieve this, three prerequisites are needed: large-scale launch capacity, energy collection and cooling systems in space, and a sufficient number of computing chips. This is the underlying logic of SpaceX's three main businesses. Starship addresses the launch capacity issue, aiming to send millions of tons of supplies into orbit annually. Starlink and space-based AI data centers address energy utilization by deploying solar panels and computing facilities in space. The Terafab chip factory addresses chip supply, as current global chip production capacity is far from sufficient. This sounds like science fiction, but SpaceX is gradually turning it into reality. Business Segment One: Starship SpaceX currently accounts for 85-90% of the world's total orbital mass. This number is astonishing in itself, but more importantly, it represents the transformative power of Starship. The Falcon 9 has already proven the commercial value of reusable rockets. It reduced launch costs from $25,000/kg during the Space Shuttle era to approximately $2,700/kg. But Starship aims for $100/kg—another order of magnitude reduction. Starship is the largest, heaviest, and most powerful spacecraft in human history. Its thrust is 2-3 times that of the Saturn V (depending on the version), and it is the first super-heavy rocket designed to be fully reusable. SpaceX even eliminated the weight of the landing legs, allowing the launch tower to directly capture the returning rocket. This extreme quality optimization aims to achieve one goal: to make rockets like airplanes, able to land, refuel, and take off again. The target operational rhythm is one launch per hour, and SpaceX can now manufacture one Starship every two weeks. Once the Gigabay factory in Texas is operational, annual production capacity will reach 1,000 ships. Why is such a high launch frequency necessary? Because SpaceX plans to deploy not a few hundred satellites, but hundreds of thousands. Starlink V3 satellites are larger and more powerful, and each Starship can carry approximately 60. To deploy 100,000 V3 satellites, even with 60 launches per launch, approximately 1,700 launches would be needed. To complete deployment within a few years, multiple launches per day would be required. More importantly, without Starship, SpaceX's other ambitious plans cannot be realized. The space AI data center needs to send a large number of solar panels, radiators, and computing devices into orbit. The lunar base needs to transport construction materials. Mars colonization is entirely dependent on the success of Starship. Starship's commercialization target is the end of 2026, but this timeline is already several years behind schedule. Recent test flights have made significant progress, but there are still some issues to be resolved with the rocket and boosters. This is the cornerstone of the entire strategy, and any further delays will affect all subsequent plans. Business Segment Two: Starlink If Starship is SpaceX's future, then Starlink is its current cash cow. As of 2025, Starlink had over 10,000 satellites in orbit, serving 10 million users and generating $11.4 billion in annual revenue. This figure was only $3.9 billion in 2023, nearly doubling in two years. More importantly, this business has demonstrated a powerful flywheel effect. Each new satellite increases the network's total capacity, enabling it to serve more users. The marginal cost of adding users is extremely low—the satellites are already in orbit, and serving an additional user incurs almost no additional cost. User payments become cash flow, which is then reinvested in research and development and the deployment of better satellites. Better satellites attract more users, accelerating the flywheel's rotation. Currently, Starlink's cost is approximately $20 per user per month, with a goal of reducing it to $10. This would bring it to half the price of traditional service providers. Moreover, with the deployment of V3 satellites, service quality will reach or even surpass fiber optic levels. Each V3 satellite can provide over 1Tbps of capacity, several times that of current satellites. SpaceX plans to eventually deploy 100,000 V3/V4/V5 satellites, ten times the current number. This will make Starlink a core part of the global internet infrastructure. Musk even mentioned that Starlink may eventually dominate internet, cloud, and AI traffic and charge for unique services such as interconnectivity. Even greater ambitions lie in the mobile market. SpaceX plans to launch a hybrid satellite-and-terrestrial network within three years, enabling direct-to-cell connections between mobile phones and satellites. This requires massive spectrum investment—estimated at around $100 billion—and significantly increased capital expenditures. By 2030, Starlink's annual CapEx could reach $35 billion. Oppenheimer predicts that Starlink will eventually capture 25% of the $2 trillion communications market and have 230 million broadband users within ten years. Whether this prediction is accurate remains to be seen, but the direction is clear: Starlink is transforming from a satellite internet service provider into a dominant player in global communications infrastructure. It's worth noting that SpaceX is currently the only company with experience operating tens of thousands of satellites. This experience cannot be accumulated overnight; it involves a series of complex systems engineering issues such as orbit management, collision avoidance, satellite updates, and ground station coordination. This is a natural competitive advantage. Business Segment Three: The Bold Gamble with xAI In early 2026, SpaceX acquired xAI for $250 billion. This price itself demonstrates Musk's emphasis on the AI ​​business. xAI is not simply an AI model developer, but a vertically integrated system attempting to cover the entire AI stack. xAI consists of six parts: X (data source), Grok (large language models), Colossus (ground data center), Cursor (AI programming tools), Terafab (chip manufacturing), and Macrohard (software system). SpaceX is about to acquire Cursor for $60 billion; this AI programming tool has tripled its revenue in the past six months, reaching an annualized revenue of $3 billion. However, the challenges facing xAI are also obvious: the capabilities of Grok models are currently about six months behind those of major competitors. Musk has publicly acknowledged this gap and stated that it needs to be caught up. In the rapidly evolving field of AI, a six-month gap can mean a lot. What is SpaceX's strategy? In the short term, it will continue to use cutting-edge models from other vendors. In the medium term, it will gain market share and user data through products like Cursor. In the long term, it will establish a leading position based on cost and capacity advantages. The core of this long-term advantage is the space-based AI data center. The AI1 satellite is SpaceX's first-generation product designed for space computing. Its specifications are clear: a peak power of 150 kilowatts and a continuous power of 120 kilowatts, equivalent to an Nvidia GB300 rack equipped with 72 GPUs. The satellite's main components are solar panels, heat sinks, and computing chips. The solar panels are designed for 250 watts per square meter, and the heat sink is designed for 1,400 watts per square meter (double-sided cooling), with a wingspan of approximately 70 meters. These technologies are already in use or will be used in Starlink V3 satellites, requiring no magical technological breakthroughs. In fact, the AI1 satellite is simpler than Starlink satellites. Starlink requires complex phased array antennas, parabolic antennas, and numerous laser links. The AI1 satellite mainly consists of solar panels, heat sinks, and chips, connecting to the Starlink constellation via laser links, from which Starlink transmits data back to Earth. What are the core advantages of a space-based data center? First, energy. Solar energy is 10 times more efficient in space than on Earth—there is no atmosphere to obstruct it, no day-night cycle, and no weather effects. Second, heat dissipation. Ground-based data centers require complex cooling systems, while in space, heat can be directly radiated into the vacuum. Third, latency. At an orbital altitude of 600-800 kilometers, the latency for light-speed transmission is only about 3 milliseconds, which is perfectly acceptable. Of course, technological challenges remain. Chips need to operate reliably in the space environment, cooling systems need to be validated, and costs need to be proven competitive. SpaceX's timeline is: reaching an annualized rate of 1 gigawatt per year by the end of 2025, 10 gigawatts per year by mid-2027, 100 gigawatts per year by mid-2028, with a long-term goal of 1 terawatt per year. For comparison, the current global cloud computing capacity is approximately 100 gigawatts. SpaceX's goal is to deploy computing power equivalent to 10 times the current global capacity within a few years—and in space. What if the progress of the space data center is not as expected? SpaceX has contingency plans. It has already built the Colossus terrestrial data center, reportedly the world's fastest and lowest-cost data center. Before space data center technology matures, Colossus can support the development of AI businesses. How many chips are needed to achieve 1 terawatt of computing power? Assuming each chip provides 1 kilowatt, that's 1 billion chips. This far exceeds the current global chip production capacity. This is the reason for Terafab's existence. This planned chip factory will cover 100 million square feet, 10 times the size of Tesla's Texas Gigafactory. It will be built in partnership with Tesla and Intel, with a target annual production capacity of 1 billion chips. Construction of Terafab has already begun. A solar panel manufacturing facility in Bastrop, Texas, is under construction, and an AI satellite production facility is about to begin construction. SpaceX expects to achieve reasonable production capacity by the end of 2025. However, even if Terafab is successfully built, it will only support terawatts. To increase capacity by another 1,000 times to petawatts, a completely different approach is needed. SpaceX's lunar program sounds like science fiction, but the technological logic is clear. Solar panels and heat sinks can be produced on-site on the moon, and chips can be transported from Earth or manufactured on the moon. What are the advantages of the moon? Gravity is only one-sixth that of Earth, and there is no atmosphere. This means that AI satellites can be accelerated to sufficient speeds using electromagnetic mass actuators—essentially giant linear motors—and launched directly into deep space without rockets. This method is far more efficient than launching from Earth. And once a production base is established on the Moon, scaling becomes relatively easy. This is the only viable path from terawatts to petawatts. Of course, this plan hinges on the success of Starship, the space data center, Terafab, and the establishment of an industrial base on the Moon. Every step is challenging, but every step is on SpaceX's roadmap. SpaceX's core competitiveness lies not only in technological innovation but also in its manufacturing capabilities. Over 80% of Starship components, Starlink satellites, and related equipment are manufactured in-house. This extreme vertical integration is uncommon in modern business, but it gives SpaceX a significant advantage: cost control, quality control, rapid iteration, and supply chain security. xAI is the only fully vertically integrated AI developer. From data on the X platform to models in Grok, to data centers in Colossus, to the future Terafab chip, and software systems in Macrohard, the entire stack is under SpaceX's control. This control is not just for profit, but to ensure that scarce resources—chips, data, and computing power—are prioritized for SpaceX's strategic goals. The Gigafactory in Bastrop, Texas, is expanding. Starlink user terminals are already being produced here, with an expected final output of hundreds of millions of units. A solar panel manufacturing facility is under construction, and an AI satellite production facility is about to begin construction. SpaceX aims to achieve reasonable production capacity by the end of 2025 to support large-scale deployments starting in 2026. SpaceX's engineering philosophy is the Silicon Valley-style "fail fast" approach: test to break, learn from failure, and iterate rapidly. Starlink has already reached version V3, and Starship is constantly being improved. This approach is not common in the traditional aerospace industry, but it allows SpaceX to advance technology at a much faster pace than its competitors. The competitive landscape is virtually unassailable. SpaceX's absolute dominance in the launch market is virtually unchallenged. It accounts for 85-90% of global orbital mass, with the remaining 10-15% mainly from China, and other countries and companies combined accounting for only 5-7%. The most likely competitor is Blue Origin. The New Glenn rocket's maiden flight was successful, but a major accident occurred subsequently, damaging the launch pad. Blue Origin stated it would repair the damage within seven months and resume flights by the end of 2026, but this timeline is considered quite aggressive. More importantly, this accident severely impacted Blue Origin's Kuiper satellite deployment plans. Kuiper is Amazon's satellite internet project, originally planned to launch a certain number of satellites by July 2026, a deadline that will now certainly be unattainable. Industry experts generally believe that Kuiper will need 3-4 years to become truly competitive in the satellite network market. Over the next 3-4 years, Starlink will continue to expand. By the time Kuiper truly reaches scale, Starlink may already hold a dominant market share, boasting tens of millions of users and a comprehensive service system. It will become much more difficult for newcomers to catch up. The small and medium-sized launch market still offers opportunities. Companies like Rocket Lab, focusing on small payloads, have established themselves in this segment. The European polar orbit launch market also has room for growth, as geographical limitations make equatorial launches difficult for European countries. However, in the heavy launch market, SpaceX's lead is likely to persist for a long time. While the US government wants to maintain competition (not putting all eggs in one basket), there are simply too few truly competitive players. The Overwhelming Advantages of Satellite Communications In the field of satellite communications, Starlink's advantages are even more pronounced. It is currently the only company with experience operating tens of thousands of satellites. This kind of experience cannot be accumulated overnight. Managing 10,000 satellites is an extremely complex systems engineering problem. It requires real-time tracking of the position and status of each satellite, orbit planning to avoid collisions, coordinating communications at ground stations, and managing satellite upgrades and replacements. SpaceX has built a complete system to handle these problems, and this system is constantly being improved. Even if Kuiper can overcome the launch delay problem, it will take time to build similar operational capabilities. In the meantime, Starlink will continue to improve its services, reduce costs, and expand its user base. Network effects amplify the advantage of leaders. In the field of AI, SpaceX is currently in a follower position. Its Grok model lags behind its main competitors by about six months, a significant gap in the rapidly evolving AI landscape. However, SpaceX possesses several unique advantages. First is the data on the X platform. Social media data is invaluable for training AI models, and X is one of the few platforms with massive amounts of real-time data. Second is the acquisition of Cursor. Cursor has experienced rapid growth in the AI ​​programming tools market, tripling its revenue in six months. This has brought not only revenue but also users and usage data. The most crucial advantage is future computing power. If the space data center is successfully deployed, SpaceX will possess the world's largest and lowest-cost computing power. In the computationally-intensive field of AI, this could be a decisive advantage. Of course, all of this hinges on the success of the space data center. If insurmountable technical or economic obstacles arise, SpaceX's AI strategy will require significant adjustments. However, even relying solely on its ground-based Colossus data center, SpaceX is capable of securing a place in the AI ​​market. Financial Performance SpaceX's financial data demonstrates a rapidly growing, highly capital-intensive business model. Revenue growth is significant. $10.4 billion in 2023, $14 billion in 2024 (35% growth), and $18.7 billion in 2025 (33% growth). Oppenheimer predicts it will reach $31.3 billion in 2026 (68% growth) and $58.8 billion in 2027 (88% growth). The business structure is rapidly changing. In 2025, Starlink accounted for 61% of revenue ($11.4 billion), launch services for 22% ($4.1 billion), and AI for 17% ($3.2 billion). By 2026, AI revenue is expected to grow by 324% to $13.7 billion, increasing its share to 44%. After 2027, AI revenue may surpass Starlink to become the largest revenue source. However, this growth requires huge investments. Capital expenditures are projected to reach $20.7 billion in 2025 and $59.9 billion in 2026, nearly tripling. Of this $59.9 billion, $49.9 billion will be invested in AI infrastructure, including space data centers, Terafab, and terrestrial data centers. Future capital needs are even more staggering. Oppenheimer estimates that achieving SpaceX's strategic goals will require approximately $1.6 trillion in cumulative capital expenditures and spectrum investments. This includes Starlink's expansion (particularly the $100 billion spectrum investment needed to enter the mobile market), Starship's mass production, the deployment of space data centers, the construction of Terafab, and more. SpaceX will also need an additional $300 billion in financing. An IPO is an important step, but far from enough. Multiple rounds of financing, bond issuance, and even mergers with Tesla or other companies may be necessary to raise more capital in the future. Regarding profitability, Oppenheimer predicts that SpaceX's revenue will exceed $200 billion by 2030, with an EBITDA margin of approximately 60%. By 2035, revenue could reach $900 billion, with EBITDA around $500 billion. These figures seem impressive, but they are based on the assumption that SpaceX can successfully execute its strategy. The Justification for $2.5 Trillion: Oppenheimer's target price of $190 corresponds to a company valuation of approximately $2.5 trillion, representing a 41% upside from the IPO price of $135. How was this valuation derived? Oppenheimer used a 10-year DCF (Discounted Cash Flow) model with a weighted average cost of capital (WACC) of 10.5% and a long-term growth rate of 5%. The model predicts that SpaceX's revenue will be approximately $900 billion, EBITDA approximately $500 billion, and terminal EBIT $220 billion by 2035. The 10.5% WACC reflects the high risks facing SpaceX. Technological, execution, market, and regulatory risks are all significant. However, if SpaceX succeeds, the returns will be enormous. The reasonableness of this valuation depends on several key assumptions: First, the market potential for the three main business segments is indeed huge. Oppenheimer estimates that by 2035, the communications market will be around $2 trillion, the AI ​​market could reach trillions of dollars, and the space economy around $800 billion. The three combined exceed $10 trillion. If SpaceX can capture a significant share of these markets, a valuation of $2.5 trillion is not unreasonable. Secondly, space infrastructure has structural cost advantages. Solar energy is 10 times more efficient in space than on Earth, heat dissipation is easier, there are no land costs, and no environmental restrictions. If these advantages can be translated into actual cost advantages, SpaceX will have a unique competitive edge in the AI ​​computing market. Thirdly, the value created by vertical integration. SpaceX controls the entire chain from rocket manufacturing, satellite production, chip manufacturing to data center operations. This control not only reduces costs but also ensures supply chain security and rapid technological iteration. Fourth, Starlink's cash flow support capability. Starlink is already a profitable business and is growing rapidly. Its cash flow can support the development of Starship and xAI, forming a self-reinforcing cycle. However, its valuation also faces scrutiny. The biggest problem is execution risk. Starship's commercialization has been delayed for several years, the space data center is not yet validated, Terafab is still in the planning stage, and AI model capabilities are still catching up. Any significant delay or failure in any of these areas could have a ripple effect on the overall strategy. Another issue is the complexity of valuing the three businesses together. Launch services, satellite communications, and AI are three completely different businesses with different risk characteristics and growth prospects. Valuing them together makes it difficult for investors to accurately assess the value of each component. There are also corporate governance issues. SpaceX is highly dependent on Elon Musk, who also manages Tesla, SpaceX, and other companies. The limited number of shares in circulation (only 3.5%) could lead to significant stock price volatility. Public market scrutiny could influence SpaceX's consistently aggressive decision-making style. Several factors will determine the future. The commercialization of Starship is the most critical variable. Starship is the cornerstone of the entire strategy; without it, Starlink V3 cannot be deployed, space data centers cannot be realized, and a lunar base is out of the question. The current timeline is to enter commercial service by the end of 2026. Recent test flights have yielded significant progress—the rocket successfully launched, entered orbit, re-entered orbit, and landed, and the booster was successfully captured by the launch pad. However, some issues remain to be addressed, including certain systems of the rocket and boosters. If Starship can be commercialized on schedule and achieve high-frequency launches in the following years, SpaceX's strategy will proceed as planned. If there are significant delays—such as a postponement to 2028 or later—all subsequent plans will need to be adjusted, and the valuation will face pressure. Key metrics to watch include: the frequency and success rate of test flights, the start date of commercial launches, the rate of increase in launch frequency, and the rate of decrease in cost per launch. Validation of Space Data Centers Space AI data centers are the most innovative and controversial part of SpaceX's strategy. Technically, key issues include the effectiveness of the cooling system, the reliable operation of chips in the space environment, and competitive cost. SpaceX plans to reach an annualized rate of 1 gigawatt per year by the end of 2025, which will be the first important validation point. If initial validation is successful, the 10 gigawatt target by mid-2027 and the 100 gigawatt target by mid-2028 will become more credible. If technical or economic obstacles are encountered, SpaceX may need more time to resolve the issues or adjust its strategy, relying more on ground-based data centers. Key metrics to watch include: the launch time and number of AI1 satellites, preliminary test results, cost data (if released), and contracts signed with large enterprise clients. The Grok model is currently about six months behind its competitors, and this gap needs to be narrowed. The acquisition of Cursor will enhance SpaceX's AI product competitiveness in the short term. Cursor is growing rapidly in the AI ​​programming tools market, which itself is expanding rapidly. If the integration goes smoothly, SpaceX will gain a strong foothold in AI applications. In the long term, SpaceX needs to prove it can develop competitive AI models. This requires not only substantial computing resources (which is SpaceX's strength), but also an excellent research team, high-quality training data, and effective training methods. Key metrics to watch include: the release and performance evaluation of new versions of Grok, Cursor's user and revenue growth, the signing of large enterprise AI contracts, and SpaceX's AI talent recruitment efforts. Starlink's Continued Expansion is currently SpaceX's most stable source of cash flow, and its continued growth is crucial to the overall strategy. The deployment of V3 satellites will be a significant milestone. Each V3 satellite has several times the capacity of current satellites and will achieve fiber-optic performance. If V3 deployment proceeds smoothly, Starlink's service quality and capacity will be significantly improved. Entering the mobile market is another crucial step. This requires significant investment in spectrum and technology development, but the potential market size is far larger than that of fixed broadband. If SpaceX can establish a foothold in the mobile market, both revenue and subscriber numbers will increase substantially. Key metrics to watch include: the V3 satellite launch schedule, subscriber growth, ARPU (average revenue per user) changes, service quality improvements, and the timeline for entering the mobile market. Terafab Construction Progress To achieve 1 terawatt of computing power, SpaceX needs its own chip supply. Terafab is a key part of this strategy. Building a 100 million square foot factory is a massive undertaking in itself. Site selection, design, construction, equipment procurement, recruitment, and capacity ramp-up—each step requires time and capital. Moreover, chip manufacturing is a highly complex process, and improving yield rates can take years. Currently, Bastrop's solar panel manufacturing facility is under construction, which is a positive sign. However, it could take several years from groundbreaking to achieving meaningful production capacity. Key metrics to monitor include: factory construction progress, equipment procurement and installation, the production time of the first chips, yield rates, and capacity ramp-up speed. The development of emerging space industries: SpaceX's long-term value lies not only in its three current businesses but also in the emerging industries it may initiate. A lunar economy is a clear direction. If SpaceX can significantly reduce the cost of Earth-Moon transportation, lunar scientific research, resource development, and even tourism could become a reality. Oppenheimer conservatively estimates that the space economy will reach $800 billion by 2035, but this figure could increase significantly if the lunar and Martian economies develop smoothly. Point-to-point rocket transportation is another possibility. Starship could theoretically transport cargo or passengers to anywhere on Earth within an hour. If this service can be commercialized, it will create an entirely new market. Concepts such as on-orbit manufacturing, space energy production, and asteroid mining currently seem far-fetched, but they may gradually become feasible as launch costs decrease. These emerging industries currently contribute little to the valuation, but they represent SpaceX's long-term potential.
